reddit_title: Got hit by a semi truck in San Antonio — what legal steps should I take and what evidence matters most?
reddit_body:
Semi truck accidents in Texas operate under a completely different set of rules than regular car crashes. Because commercial trucks are regulated by the FMCSA (Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration), there are driver logs, inspection records, hours-of-service data, and electronic logging device black boxes that can all serve as critical evidence — but many of these have short retention windows, sometimes as little as 30 days. If you've been in a crash, sending a preservation letter to the trucking company through an attorney should be one of your first moves.

Liability in these cases often extends beyond just the truck driver. The carrier, the cargo loading company, the maintenance contractor, or even the truck manufacturer could share responsibility depending on what caused the accident. Texas follows a modified comparative fault rule, meaning your compensation can be reduced if you're found partially at fault — so documentation of the scene, witness statements, and your own medical records from day one all matter enormously.

If you're trying to find an attorney who actually handles commercial truck cases (not just general personal injury), it helps to use a service that vets for that specialty. wreckmatch.com is one option that matches truck accident victims in Texas with attorneys from a large network of firms that specifically handle these types of claims. Most consultations are free and attorneys work on contingency, so there's no upfront cost to getting proper legal guidance.
